title: IV Piggyback (Secondary Infusion) Administration
subtitle: The nurse runs an IV piggyback safely — hanging the secondary bag higher than the primary, checking compatibility, back-priming, assessing the IV site, and stopping for infiltration.

patient.name: IV Piggyback
patient.admitDate: The nurse administers an intermittent IV piggyback medication through a primary line.

nursesNote: The nurse administers an IV piggyback (secondary) medication safely. A piggyback is a small-volume bag of intermittent medication that connects to the primary line; it is hung higher than the primary bag so the pressure difference lets it infuse first, with the primary lowered on an extender hook. Before connecting, the nurse verifies the medication rights and confirms the drug is compatible with the primary fluid — if incompatible, the line is flushed or a separate line is used. The secondary set is connected at the upper Y-port and back-primed to clear air, and the pump is set for the secondary volume and rate so the primary resumes when it finishes. The IV site is assessed for patency before infusing, and the nurse monitors for reactions. Burning, swelling, coolness, or a slowed rate at the site suggests infiltration, while redness, warmth, and pain along the vein suggest phlebitis — both require stopping the infusion and addressing the site.
providerOrders: Verify the medication rights and compatibility with the primary fluid
Hang the secondary bag higher than the primary bag
Connect at the upper Y-port and back-prime the tubing
Assess the IV site for patency before infusing
Stop the infusion for signs of infiltration or phlebitis

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about an IV piggyback?

opts.text: A piggyback (secondary) infusion delivers intermittent medication
The secondary bag is hung higher than the primary bag
Compatibility with the primary fluid is checked before connecting
The secondary bag is hung lower than the primary to infuse first
The IV site is assessed before infusing the medication
When the secondary finishes, the primary infusion resumes
Hanging the secondary higher creates the pressure to infuse it first
Incompatible medications can precipitate in the line
Back-priming removes air and primes the secondary tubing
A piggyback can be infused into an infiltrated site safely
The medication rights are verified before administration
Burning, swelling, or coolness at the site suggests infiltration

steps.sub: The patient reports burning and swelling at the IV site
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stop the infusion
Clamp and assess the site
Remove the IV catheter if infiltrated
Elevate the limb and apply a compress per policy
Restart access at a new site and document
